title: Defending Against a Phishing Attack
description: You are a Blue Team SOC analyst. A spear-phishing campaign is targeting your CFO. Your decisions determine the outcome — early action prevents damage, hesitation is costly. Three paths through this scenario reflect real-world IR choices.

phases.name: Phase 1: Initial Detection
phases.narrative: Your SIEM triggers an alert at 08:42: multiple failed O365 logins on the CFO's account from a TOR exit node in Romania. Simultaneously, the email gateway flags an inbound message with a macro-enabled Excel attachment that failed DMARC authentication. EDR shows DESKTOP-CFO01 as idle. What is your first action?
phases.hint: Consider what stops the attack soonest. Proactive user communication can be faster than firewall changes and prevents execution entirely. Technical controls are important but so is getting to the human first.

logs.event: Failed login - user: jsmith@company.com - IP: 185.220.101.45 (TOR exit node) - Location: Romania
Failed login attempt 2/5 - jsmith@company.com - IP: 185.220.101.45
Inbound email flagged: Subject='Invoice #4521 Urgent' - Attachment: Invoice_4521.xlsm - Sender: vendor@acme-supplies.net (DMARC FAIL)
Warning: macro-enabled attachment delivered to jsmith@company.com — not yet opened

choices.text: 📞 Call the CFO immediately and warn them not to open any suspicious email attachment
choices.outcome: Outstanding proactive response! You reach the CFO before they open the email. They confirm receiving a suspicious invoice but haven't clicked it. You have stopped the attack before any code executed — the best possible outcome. Now you can focus on eliminating the threat from your environment.

choices.text: 🔒 Block the suspicious TOR exit-node IP at the firewall and investigate the flagged email
choices.outcome: Good technical response. The IP is blocked quickly. However, while you were configuring the firewall rule, the CFO opened the attachment and enabled macros. DESKTOP-CFO01 is now connecting to the attacker's C2 server on port 4444. You have an active breach to contain.

choices.text: 👁️ Continue passive monitoring to gather more threat intelligence before acting
choices.outcome: The passive approach costs critical minutes. While you monitor, the CFO opens the macro-enabled attachment and enables content. The attacker immediately establishes a reverse shell and begins lateral movement and data exfiltration. You now have a full, escalating breach.

phases.name: Phase 2: Proactive Containment
phases.narrative: The CFO has been warned and has not opened the attachment. Your email gateway analysis confirms the attachment is a macro dropper attempting to call back to 185.220.101.45:4444. SIEM shows no malicious activity on DESKTOP-CFO01 — the endpoint is clean. A search reveals 4 other employees received near-identical emails. How do you eliminate this threat from your environment?
phases.hint: Since no malware has executed, your priority is removing the threat from ALL affected mailboxes — not just the CFO's. An email search-and-purge across the organisation is the highest-impact action here.

logs.event: Attachment analysis: Invoice_4521.xlsm — VBA macro dropper — C2: 185.220.101.45:4444
IP 185.220.101.45 attributed to 9 active phishing campaigns (ThreatFox, AlienVault OTX)
Organisation-wide search: 4 additional recipients of identical campaign email found
DESKTOP-CFO01 status: CLEAN — no suspicious process activity detected

choices.text: 🗑️ Search and purge the malicious email from all mailboxes, block the sender domain, and update threat intel feeds
choices.outcome: Excellent proactive response! The email is purged from all 5 affected mailboxes before any user opens it. The attacker's sending domain is blocked. IOCs are shared with your threat intel platform to protect the wider community. Threat fully neutralised — zero infection.

choices.text: 🚫 Block the sender's email address and advise the CFO to delete the email
choices.outcome: Partial response. The CFO's email is deleted and the sender is blocked going forward. However, you didn't sweep for the 4 other recipients. One of them opens the attachment 12 minutes later — a secondary incident is now developing alongside your ongoing investigation.

choices.text: 📢 Draft and send a company-wide phishing warning email
choices.outcome: Too slow and too generic. Composing and sending a mass email takes 20 minutes. By that time, two other employees have opened the malicious attachment on their workstations. Your proactive advantage has been partially squandered.

phases.name: Phase 2: Active Breach Containment
phases.narrative: EDR confirms DESKTOP-CFO01 is compromised. Process telemetry shows: EXCEL.EXE → cmd.exe → powershell.exe with a base64-encoded payload. An outbound C2 connection is established on port 4444. A registry run key has been added for persistence and a scheduled task named 'MicrosoftEdgeUpdate' was created. The CFO is on the phone asking what is happening. What do you do first?
phases.hint: Network isolation cuts the attacker's access immediately. But don't reimage before capturing a forensic snapshot — you need evidence to understand the full scope of compromise, including what was accessed and what other systems the attacker may have touched.

logs.event: OUTBOUND ALLOWED - src: 10.0.1.50 (DESKTOP-CFO01) → dst: 185.220.101.45:4444 - TCP ESTABLISHED - 2.1KB
Process tree: EXCEL.EXE → cmd.exe → powershell.exe -WindowStyle Hidden -enc JABjAG...
Registry: HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run\WindowsUpdate = powershell.exe -enc [payload]
Scheduled task created: 'MicrosoftEdgeUpdate' — runs hourly (secondary persistence mechanism)

choices.text: 🔌 Network-isolate DESKTOP-CFO01, disable the CFO's O365 account, and preserve a forensic memory image
choices.outcome: Optimal containment! Network isolation cuts the C2 channel within seconds. Account disable prevents the attacker from abusing the stolen O365 session. Forensic memory image preserves evidence for scope analysis. You have contained the threat professionally.

choices.text: 🔧 Block outbound port 4444 at the perimeter firewall
choices.outcome: Partial containment only. You block port 4444 but the attacker immediately pivots — the PowerShell beacon switches to port 443 (HTTPS), which is indistinguishable from normal web traffic. The C2 channel is re-established in under 90 seconds. The endpoint and O365 account remain at risk.

choices.text: 💿 Immediately reimage the workstation to restore it to a clean baseline
choices.outcome: Too hasty. While reimaging removes the malware, you've destroyed all forensic evidence — volatile memory, process artefacts, and the attack timeline are gone. You cannot determine what data was exfiltrated, what other systems were accessed, or the full scope of compromise. A forensic snapshot first costs only minutes.

phases.name: Phase 2: Escalated Breach — Active Threat
phases.narrative: CRITICAL: The attacker has been active on DESKTOP-CFO01 for 11 minutes. EDR shows lateral movement attempts via SMB to FILE-SERVER-01. O365 audit logs confirm the CFO's mailbox was accessed: 47 emails read, 2 SharePoint files downloaded including 'Q4_Budget_2024.xlsx'. A Kerberoasting attempt detected — 6 service account tickets requested. This is a major incident. What is your immediate priority?
phases.hint: Stop the bleeding first — at this scope, multiple systems may be compromised. Proportional response means declaring a major incident, isolating more than just the initial endpoint, and escalating to leadership and your IR team. Don't try to handle this alone.

logs.event: Lateral movement: SMB authentication attempts from DESKTOP-CFO01 to FILE-SERVER-01 (3 attempts, 1 success)
Suspicious session: 47 emails accessed, files downloaded — 'Q4_Budget_2024.xlsx', 'Supplier_Contracts.docx'
Kerberoasting: TGS tickets requested for svc_backup, svc_db, svc_print, svc_deploy, svc_report, svc_scan
ALERT: IOC overlap with threat actor 'TempestFox' — known financial sector campaign, active since 2023

choices.text: 🚨 Declare major incident — isolate CFO machine and file server, escalate to CISO and IR team, reset all targeted service accounts
choices.outcome: Correct and proportional response. Major incident declared. CISO and IR team engaged. Both DESKTOP-CFO01 and FILE-SERVER-01 are isolated, cutting lateral movement. Service accounts are reset before offline cracking can succeed. The response is appropriate to the severity.

choices.text: 🔌 Isolate DESKTOP-CFO01 only and monitor the file server for further activity
choices.outcome: Insufficient scope. You contain the initial host but the file server — which received a successful SMB connection — is left running. The attacker has already established a foothold there. A service account with a weak password is cracked offline six hours later, enabling re-entry.

choices.text: ⏳ Wait for management sign-off before taking any disruptive containment action
choices.outcome: Fatal delay. The 25 minutes spent waiting for approval allows the attacker to complete lateral movement, compromise the file server, and dump LSASS credentials for domain accounts. This escalates from a phishing incident to a potential domain compromise. Leadership will be far less forgiving of this delay than of disruptive but necessary containment.

phases.name: Phase 3: Hardening After Early Interception
phases.narrative: No malware executed — you stopped the attack before it caused damage. Now it is time to close the security gaps that allowed this phishing email to be delivered in the first place. Threat analysis reveals the email bypassed your gateway because DMARC is in monitoring mode only ('p=none'). Your security team confirms 23% of users are not enrolled in MFA. How do you harden defences?
phases.hint: Address both the technical root causes (DMARC policy, macro settings) and the human element (MFA, user training). Single controls are good; layered controls are excellent.

logs.event: Root cause: DMARC policy 'p=none' (monitor only) — spoofed sender was not rejected
MFA enrollment: 77% complete — 23% of accounts remain MFA-exempt
Macro policy: All Office macros allowed for all users from all sources — no GPO restriction

choices.text: 🛡️ Enforce DMARC reject policy, mandate MFA for all O365 accounts, disable macros via Group Policy, and schedule a phishing simulation within 30 days
choices.outcome: Exemplary security hardening. DMARC reject closes the email spoofing gap that allowed delivery. MFA protects credentials even if future emails succeed. GPO macro restriction eliminates this entire attack class from endpoints. A phishing simulation will benchmark user resilience. Full-spectrum response.

choices.text: 🔐 Mandate MFA enrolment for all remaining O365 accounts immediately
choices.outcome: MFA is the single highest-impact control and this is a strong action. It protects accounts even if credentials are later phished. However, DMARC remains in monitoring mode and Office macros remain unrestricted — the specific delivery mechanism in this attack is still viable against other users.

choices.text: 📧 Send a phishing awareness bulletin to all staff
choices.outcome: Awareness training is valuable but addresses only the human element. All three technical root causes — permissive DMARC policy, partial MFA, and unrestricted macros — remain unchanged. The next well-crafted phishing email would face identical defences.

phases.name: Phase 3: Eradication
phases.narrative: DESKTOP-CFO01 is isolated. Forensic analysis identifies two persistence mechanisms: a registry run key and an hourly scheduled task named 'MicrosoftEdgeUpdate'. O365 audit logs show the attacker accessed 47 emails and downloaded two SharePoint files during the 8 minutes before containment. The attacker's session token may still be valid. How do you fully eradicate this threat?
phases.hint: Eradication must be complete — missing even one persistence mechanism means re-compromise. The O365 account needs full session revocation, not just a password reset. Check for ALL persistence artefacts before declaring the system clean.

logs.event: Exfiltrated files: 'Q4_Budget_2024.xlsx' (SharePoint), 'Supplier_Contracts.docx' (SharePoint) — via session token still active
Persistence #1: HKCU\Run\WindowsUpdate = powershell.exe -WindowStyle Hidden -enc [encoded payload]
Persistence #2: Scheduled task 'MicrosoftEdgeUpdate' — runs hourly, hidden, calls %TEMP%\svchost32.exe
Malware artefacts: %TEMP%\svchost32.exe, C:\Users\jsmith\AppData\Roaming\.config (staging directory)

choices.text: 🧹 Reset O365 password and revoke all active sessions, remove both persistence mechanisms, delete malware artefacts, reimage the workstation from golden image
choices.outcome: Thorough and systematic. Both persistence mechanisms are removed so there is no re-entry path. O365 sessions are fully revoked — the attacker cannot abuse the stolen token. The workstation is rebuilt from a known-clean image. Excellent, complete eradication.

choices.text: 🔑 Reset the CFO's O365 password and remove the registry run key
choices.outcome: Incomplete eradication. You address one persistence mechanism but miss the scheduled task. Exactly 1 hour later, 'MicrosoftEdgeUpdate' fires, re-downloads the implant, and re-establishes the C2 channel. The attacker regains access — you now have a re-compromise event to explain to the CISO.

choices.text: 🦠 Run a full antivirus scan and remove detected threats, then bring the machine back online
choices.outcome: AV removes the known malware binary but misses the scheduled task (fileless, stored in the registry) and the encoded PowerShell payload. The system shows clean in AV reports but both persistence mechanisms remain active. False sense of security leads to re-compromise.

phases.name: Phase 3: Eradication Without Forensics
phases.narrative: The workstation has been reimaged — no malware remains on the endpoint. But you have no forensic telemetry from the 8 minutes the attacker was active. O365 audit logs show the attacker's session token is still valid and currently active. They have added an email forwarding rule sending all CFO correspondence to an external ProtonMail address. You are operating with a critical evidence gap. What is your next move?
phases.hint: Even without endpoint forensics, cloud audit logs are a goldmine. You need to revoke all O365 sessions immediately — the active session is a live threat. Then audit every configuration change made during the attacker's session window.

logs.event: Session ACTIVE: jsmith@company.com authenticated from IP 185.220.101.45 — token valid for 23 more hours
Forwarding rule created: All inbound/outbound CFO email → external.attacker@protonmail.com (rule active NOW)
FORENSIC GAP: No endpoint telemetry available from 08:45:00–08:48:00 — 3-minute investigation blind spot
Files accessed during gap: 'Q4_Budget_2024.xlsx', 'Supplier_Contracts.docx' — download status unknown

choices.text: ☁️ Revoke all O365 sessions immediately, reset password, audit and remove all malicious forwarding rules and inbox rules
choices.outcome: Smart pivot to cloud-based incident response. Active session revoked — attacker loses O365 access instantly. You discover and remove the email forwarding rule before any sensitive emails are forwarded. Cloud audit logs provide partial coverage of the gap period. Strong recovery from a difficult position.

choices.text: 🖥️ Rebuild the workstation and restore the user's profile from backup
choices.outcome: You clean the endpoint (which was already reimaged) but ignore the active O365 threat. The email forwarding rule continues silently copying every email the CFO sends or receives. Sensitive communications are exfiltrated for hours until an unrelated audit discovers the rule. A costly miss.

phases.name: Phase 3: Major Incident Eradication
phases.narrative: You are managing a multi-system compromise. DESKTOP-CFO01 confirmed compromised. FILE-SERVER-01 shows signs of successful lateral movement. LSASS credential dump attempted. Six service account Kerberoasting tickets requested — those passwords may be cracked offline within hours. The IR team has arrived and is ready. What is your eradication strategy?
phases.hint: At this scope, assume breach across everything that touched the compromised systems. Credential resets are time-critical when Kerberoasting occurred — offline cracking can crack weak passwords in minutes to hours. Legal and compliance teams must be looped in for potential data breach notification obligations.

logs.event: Scope assessment: DESKTOP-CFO01 (confirmed), FILE-SERVER-01 (lateral movement confirmed), DC-01 (SMB probe — under investigation)
LSASS dump: Memory access to lsass.exe detected on DESKTOP-CFO01 pre-isolation — credential theft likely
Kerberoasting: 6 service account TGS tickets captured — svc_backup and svc_db use weak passwords (policy violation)
Data exfiltration confirmed: ~2.1 MB transferred to 185.220.101.45 — financial documents involved

choices.text: 🔴 Reset all 6 service account passwords immediately, force domain-wide password reset for affected users, conduct forensics on both servers, notify Legal for breach assessment
choices.outcome: Comprehensive and proportional response. Service accounts secured before offline cracking can succeed. Domain accounts reset as a precaution given the credential dump. Legal team engaged for potential regulatory disclosure assessment. Full forensic investigation underway across affected systems. Professional major incident management.

choices.text: 🎯 Clean DESKTOP-CFO01, reset the CFO's account, and monitor the file server for further suspicious activity
choices.outcome: Scope is too narrow. The file server, which received a confirmed SMB lateral movement, is left in a monitored-but-not-remediated state. Four hours later, the svc_backup account — cracked offline from the Kerberoasting ticket — is used to log in remotely and establish persistence on the file server. Re-compromise.

phases.name: Phase 4: Recovery & Hardening
phases.narrative: The threat is contained and eradicated. Operations are being restored. Your CISO wants a comprehensive written remediation plan to present to the board. Similar phishing emails were found in 8 additional inboxes across the organisation — this was a broad campaign. What hardening measures do you prioritise to prevent recurrence?
phases.hint: The most impactful controls address root causes: email authentication (DMARC), credential protection (MFA), endpoint restriction (macro policy), and detection capability (ATP safe attachments). Layered defence-in-depth is better than any single control.

logs.event: Campaign scope: 13 emails delivered to 9 users across 3 departments — same sender infrastructure
IOCs shared to FS-ISAC and sector peers — 3 partner organisations confirmed same campaign targeting them
DMARC still p=none, 23% MFA gap, macro policy unrestricted — all three gaps remain open

choices.text: 📋 Enforce DMARC reject, mandate 100% MFA enrolment, disable macros via GPO, deploy ATP Safe Attachments for sandbox detonation, run phishing simulation within 30 days
choices.outcome: Outstanding security programme. DMARC reject eliminates spoofed sender delivery. MFA protects every credential. GPO macro restriction removes this attack class from endpoints entirely. ATP Safe Attachments sandboxes every attachment before delivery. A follow-on simulation validates effectiveness. Multi-layered, comprehensive hardening.

choices.text: 🔐 Disable Office macros via Group Policy and enforce MFA across all accounts
choices.outcome: Strong technical hardening addressing the two highest-impact controls. Macros disabled eliminates the delivery mechanism used in this attack. MFA protects credentials. DMARC and advanced threat protection are not addressed but the response is solid and substantially raises the bar for attackers.

choices.text: 📧 Issue updated phishing awareness training and an acceptable use policy reminder
choices.outcome: Awareness training is beneficial but all three technical root causes remain open. DMARC is still in monitoring mode, macros are still enabled, and MFA gaps persist. A well-crafted phishing email would face exactly the same technical defences next time.

phases.name: Phase 4: Major Incident Recovery
phases.narrative: This has been a significant security breach. Financial data is confirmed exfiltrated. Regulatory notification timelines are running — GDPR's 72-hour window has started. The CISO is presenting to the board tomorrow. Beyond technical remediation, what are the critical next steps for the organisation?
phases.hint: A confirmed data breach triggers both technical remediation AND legal/regulatory obligations. Don't let the technical response crowd out compliance requirements. Engage Legal and HR, notify regulators within required windows, and consider whether external forensics provides better credibility with regulators.

logs.event: Data breach assessment: Financial data of 3rd parties confirmed exfiltrated — GDPR Article 33 notification required within 72h
Board presentation required tomorrow morning — need incident timeline, scope, remediation plan, regulatory posture
Forensic timeline complete: initial access 08:44 → lateral movement 08:52 → exfiltration 08:53–08:55 → containment 09:00 (11 minutes dwell time)

choices.text: ⚖️ File regulatory notification, engage external forensics firm, implement zero-trust network access controls, commission a full penetration test and security audit
choices.outcome: Comprehensive major incident response. Regulatory obligations met within the 72-hour window. External forensics provides independent validation and credibility with regulators. Zero-trust implementation prevents this attack class in future. The penetration test will identify remaining gaps. Professional, thorough, and legally sound.

choices.text: 🔒 Conduct internal investigation, implement technical hardening, and prepare CISO board presentation
choices.outcome: Technical response is solid but regulatory obligations are missed. Failing to notify regulators within GDPR's 72-hour window creates additional liability. External scrutiny through independent forensics would have been more credible with regulators and insurers. A costly oversight alongside an otherwise good technical response.

phases.name: Incident Resolved
phases.narrative: You have completed the phishing incident response scenario. Your decisions shaped every aspect of this incident — from the speed and effectiveness of your initial detection through to the depth of eradication and the strength of your hardening measures. Review your decision breakdown to understand where you excelled and where improvements can be made.

endings.title: Elite Incident Responder
endings.description: Exceptional performance across every phase. You stopped the attack early, contained it precisely, eradicated completely, and implemented comprehensive hardening. Your organisation is measurably more secure as a result of your actions.

endings.title: Skilled SOC Analyst
endings.description: Strong incident response throughout. You handled the core challenges well and made mostly sound decisions. A few missed opportunities — review the decisions that cost points to sharpen your skills.

endings.title: Developing Analyst
endings.description: You navigated through the incident with some difficulty. Key containment or eradication steps were missed, allowing the attacker longer dwell time. Study the optimal paths through this scenario to improve.

endings.title: Needs Improvement
endings.description: Critical mistakes allowed the attacker significant access and dwell time. Review each phase carefully, particularly the branching paths triggered by your early decisions. Every wrong turn made the situation harder.
